items.passage:
The spacing effect is a learning phenomenon in which material reviewed across separated sessions is remembered far better than the same material crammed into a single sitting. Early investigators in the nineteenth century treated memory as a straightforward product of repetition, assuming that each additional review automatically strengthened a fixed mental trace. This assumption encouraged rote drilling, in which learners repeated facts continuously until recall felt effortless. [■]

This repetition-based model weakened once researchers began to measure how rapidly massed practice faded. Controlled studies of retention showed that cramming produced strong immediate recall that collapsed within a few days, whereas the same effort spread over weeks left durable memories. [■] Psychologists then argued that separation itself was the active ingredient, because each delayed review forced the learner to reconstruct a fading memory. [■]

This descriptive account gave way to a modern mechanistic explanation drawn from cognitive neuroscience. Researchers proposed that spaced retrieval repeatedly reactivates the neural circuits encoding a memory, and each reactivation triggers molecular changes that stabilize the connection. [■] However, this consolidation account still struggles to predict the ideal interval for any given learner, and the optimal spacing schedule appears to shift with the material and the age of the student.
paragraphs: The spacing effect is a learning phenomenon in which material reviewed across separated sessions is remembered far better than the same material crammed into a single sitting. Early investigators in the nineteenth century treated memory as a straightforward product of repetition, assuming that each additional review automatically strengthened a fixed mental trace. This assumption encouraged rote drilling, in which learners repeated facts continuously until recall felt effortless. [■]

This repetition-based model weakened once researchers began to measure how rapidly massed practice faded. Controlled studies of retention showed that cramming produced strong immediate recall that collapsed within a few days, whereas the same effort spread over weeks left durable memories. [■] Psychologists then argued that separation itself was the active ingredient, because each delayed review forced the learner to reconstruct a fading memory. [■]

This descriptive account gave way to a modern mechanistic explanation drawn from cognitive neuroscience. Researchers proposed that spaced retrieval repeatedly reactivates the neural circuits encoding a memory, and each reactivation triggers molecular changes that stabilize the connection. [■] However, this consolidation account still struggles to predict the ideal interval for any given learner, and the optimal spacing schedule appears to shift with the material and the age of the student.

questions.stem: The word "durable" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: lasting
options.B: flexible
options.C: recent
options.D: hidden

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts memories that "collapsed within a few days" with those that "left durable memories," so durable means lasting or enduring.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did controlled studies of retention show about cramming?

options.A: It produced strong recall at first that faded within a few days.
options.B: It produced weak early recall that steadily improved over weeks.
options.C: It helped learners who read facts aloud more than silent readers.
options.D: It worked better for older students than for younger ones.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "cramming produced strong immediate recall that collapsed within a few days," which option A paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the repetition-based model?

options.A: It had already identified the biological cause of durable memory.
options.B: It was based mainly on studies of very young children.
options.C: It could describe when spacing helped but not why it did.
options.D: It denied that repetition has any effect on memory at all.

questions.explanation: The paragraph shows the model was weakened by measurements it could not explain and describes the benefit as an "active ingredient" without a cause, implying it described when spacing helped but not why.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention rote drilling in paragraph 1?

options.A: To argue that repetition has no measurable effect on memory.
options.B: To show that early researchers had already discovered the spacing effect.
options.C: To describe a specific method used to test retention in laboratories.
options.D: To illustrate how the early view of memory shaped everyday learning practice.

questions.explanation: Rote drilling is introduced as what the repetition assumption "encouraged," illustrating how the early view of memory shaped how people actually studied.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a student who reviews a vocabulary list once a week for a month recalls far more of it than one who studies the same list for four hours in a single evening.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence is a concrete example of the claim just before Location 2, that effort "spread over weeks left durable memories" while cramming collapsed, contrasting spaced and massed study directly.

items.passage:
A heat engine is any device that converts a portion of thermal energy into mechanical work by allowing heat to flow from a hot region to a cold one. The operation of every such engine is governed by the second law of thermodynamics, which states that heat naturally moves from higher to lower temperatures and never spontaneously reverses. Because of this one-way tendency, no engine can transform all of its incoming heat into work, and some energy must always be released to a cooler reservoir. [■]

This unavoidable loss sets a firm ceiling on efficiency that engineers cannot exceed. The French engineer Sadi Carnot showed that the maximum possible efficiency depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs, not on the working substance used. [■] The wider the temperature gap between the two reservoirs, the greater the fraction of heat that can be converted into useful work. [■]

These principles explain why practical engines are designed to run as hot as their materials can safely allow. Power plants, for instance, raise steam to extreme temperatures precisely to widen the gap that Carnot identified. [■] However, real engines fall well short of the Carnot limit, because friction, imperfect insulation, and rapid cycling introduce losses that the idealized model deliberately ignores.
paragraphs: A heat engine is any device that converts a portion of thermal energy into mechanical work by allowing heat to flow from a hot region to a cold one. The operation of every such engine is governed by the second law of thermodynamics, which states that heat naturally moves from higher to lower temperatures and never spontaneously reverses. Because of this one-way tendency, no engine can transform all of its incoming heat into work, and some energy must always be released to a cooler reservoir. [■]

This unavoidable loss sets a firm ceiling on efficiency that engineers cannot exceed. The French engineer Sadi Carnot showed that the maximum possible efficiency depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs, not on the working substance used. [■] The wider the temperature gap between the two reservoirs, the greater the fraction of heat that can be converted into useful work. [■]

These principles explain why practical engines are designed to run as hot as their materials can safely allow. Power plants, for instance, raise steam to extreme temperatures precisely to widen the gap that Carnot identified. [■] However, real engines fall well short of the Carnot limit, because friction, imperfect insulation, and rapid cycling introduce losses that the idealized model deliberately ignores.

questions.stem: The word "unavoidable" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: optional
options.B: inevitable
options.C: sudden
options.D: minor

questions.explanation: The loss is described in paragraph 1 as something that "must always" occur, so "unavoidable" means inevitable.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, the maximum efficiency of a heat engine depends on

options.A: the temperatures of its hot and cold reservoirs.
options.B: the particular working substance used inside it.
options.C: the speed at which it completes each cycle.
options.D: the total amount of fuel it consumes.

questions.explanation: Carnot "showed that the maximum possible efficiency depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs, not on the working substance."

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: A well-insulated engine can convert all of its heat into work.
options.B: Heat naturally flows from hotter regions to cooler ones.
options.C: A larger temperature difference allows more heat to become work.
options.D: Real engines perform below the ideal efficiency limit.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "no engine can transform all of its incoming heat into work," so option A is not supported; the others are all stated.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about real engines?

options.A: They typically exceed the maximum efficiency that Carnot's model predicts.
options.B: They were first constructed long before Carnot's theoretical model appeared.
options.C: Reducing friction and heat loss would raise their efficiency toward the limit.
options.D: They cannot operate at very high temperatures without their parts melting.

questions.explanation: Because friction and insulation losses are what make real engines fall short of the Carnot limit, reducing those losses would move them closer to that ideal.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a modern gas turbine may run near 1,500 degrees Celsius, hot enough to soften many metals, in order to squeeze out extra efficiency.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a concrete example of the point just before Location 4, that power plants "raise steam to extreme temperatures" to widen the reservoir gap Carnot identified.

items.passage:
A mosaic is a picture or pattern produced by fitting together many small pieces of colored stone, glass, or ceramic, known as tesserae, onto a prepared surface. Unlike a painting, whose colors blend on a continuous surface, a mosaic builds its image from thousands of separate fragments set into mortar. Each tessera contributes only a single point of color, and the full picture emerges only when the pieces are viewed together from a distance. [■]

This piece-by-piece construction gives the medium its distinctive optical character. Because the tesserae are set at slightly different angles, their surfaces catch and scatter light unevenly, making the image shimmer as the viewer moves. [■] Skilled craftsmen exploited this property deliberately, tilting reflective glass pieces so that gold and colored backgrounds seemed to glow. [■]

These qualities made mosaic especially valued for the interiors of important buildings. In Byzantine churches, vast mosaic surfaces covered walls and domes, turning ordinary light into an atmosphere of the sacred. [■] However, the technique demanded enormous labor and expense, and it gradually declined as fresco painting offered a faster and cheaper way to decorate large surfaces.
paragraphs: A mosaic is a picture or pattern produced by fitting together many small pieces of colored stone, glass, or ceramic, known as tesserae, onto a prepared surface. Unlike a painting, whose colors blend on a continuous surface, a mosaic builds its image from thousands of separate fragments set into mortar. Each tessera contributes only a single point of color, and the full picture emerges only when the pieces are viewed together from a distance. [■]

This piece-by-piece construction gives the medium its distinctive optical character. Because the tesserae are set at slightly different angles, their surfaces catch and scatter light unevenly, making the image shimmer as the viewer moves. [■] Skilled craftsmen exploited this property deliberately, tilting reflective glass pieces so that gold and colored backgrounds seemed to glow. [■]

These qualities made mosaic especially valued for the interiors of important buildings. In Byzantine churches, vast mosaic surfaces covered walls and domes, turning ordinary light into an atmosphere of the sacred. [■] However, the technique demanded enormous labor and expense, and it gradually declined as fresco painting offered a faster and cheaper way to decorate large surfaces.

questions.stem: The word "distinctive" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: hidden
options.B: characteristic
options.C: fading
options.D: accidental

questions.explanation: The passage attributes to the medium an "optical character" all its own, so "distinctive" means characteristic or special to it.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how does a mosaic differ from a painting?

options.A: A mosaic uses colored mortar to blend its tones smoothly.
options.B: A mosaic can only show geometric patterns, not figures.
options.C: A mosaic takes far less time to complete than a painting.
options.D: A mosaic forms its image from separate fragments rather than blended color.

questions.explanation: The passage says that unlike a painting "whose colors blend on a continuous surface, a mosaic builds its image from thousands of separate fragments."

questions.stem: What does "their surfaces" refer to in paragraph 2?

options.A: the tesserae
options.B: the viewers
options.C: the buildings
options.D: the backgrounds

questions.explanation: The phrase follows "the tesserae are set at slightly different angles," so "their surfaces" refers to the tesserae.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about fresco painting?

options.A: It was more expensive than mosaic decoration.
options.B: Economic factors influenced which decorative technique was chosen.
options.C: It was invented specifically to replace mosaic in churches.
options.D: It could not be used on walls or domes.

questions.explanation: Because mosaic "declined as fresco painting offered a faster and cheaper way" to decorate, cost and effort clearly influenced which technique was used.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, a mosaic of a saint's robe might use dozens of shades of blue glass, each angled to catch the light as worshippers walked past.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Location 1
options.B: Location 2
options.C: Location 3
options.D: Location 4

questions.explanation: The sentence is a concrete illustration of the point just before Location 3, that craftsmen deliberately "tilted reflective glass pieces" so surfaces would catch the light.

items.passage:
An economy of scale is a cost advantage that a business gains when producing a good in larger quantities lowers the average cost of each unit. As output rises, the fixed costs of running a firm, such as factories, machinery, and management, are spread across more units of product. The result is that each additional item carries a smaller share of these fixed expenses, so the average cost per unit falls.

This declining cost curve arises from several distinct sources. Large producers can buy raw materials in bulk at discounted prices, invest in specialized equipment that smaller rivals cannot justify, and divide labor into efficient, repeatable tasks. Each of these factors allows the firm to convert a given quantity of inputs into a larger quantity of output.

These advantages help explain why some industries come to be dominated by a few very large firms. When average costs keep falling with size, a big producer can undercut smaller competitors and capture an ever-larger share of the market. However, scale can eventually work against a firm, because very large organizations may become slow and difficult to coordinate, and beyond a certain point average costs begin to rise again.
paragraphs: An economy of scale is a cost advantage that a business gains when producing a good in larger quantities lowers the average cost of each unit. As output rises, the fixed costs of running a firm, such as factories, machinery, and management, are spread across more units of product. The result is that each additional item carries a smaller share of these fixed expenses, so the average cost per unit falls.

This declining cost curve arises from several distinct sources. Large producers can buy raw materials in bulk at discounted prices, invest in specialized equipment that smaller rivals cannot justify, and divide labor into efficient, repeatable tasks. Each of these factors allows the firm to convert a given quantity of inputs into a larger quantity of output.

These advantages help explain why some industries come to be dominated by a few very large firms. When average costs keep falling with size, a big producer can undercut smaller competitors and capture an ever-larger share of the market. However, scale can eventually work against a firm, because very large organizations may become slow and difficult to coordinate, and beyond a certain point average costs begin to rise again.

questions.stem: The word "undercut" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: imitate
options.B: join
options.C: underprice
options.D: delay

questions.explanation: A firm with falling average costs "can undercut smaller competitors and capture" market share, meaning it can sell at a lower price, that is, underprice them.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, why does the average cost per unit fall as output rises?

options.A: Because the firm raises the price charged for each unit sold.
options.B: Because workers are paid less as production increases.
options.C: Because fixed costs are shared among a greater number of units.
options.D: Because larger firms pay lower taxes on their products.

questions.explanation: The passage explains that as output rises fixed costs "are spread across more units," so "each additional item carries a smaller share of these fixed expenses."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about small producers?

options.A: They often cannot achieve the same low unit costs as large firms.
options.B: They can usually buy their materials more cheaply than large firms.
options.C: They tend to produce goods of higher quality than large firms.
options.D: They cannot survive in any competitive market.

questions.explanation: The paragraph lists advantages available to large producers, including equipment "that smaller rivals cannot justify," implying small producers cannot reach the same low costs.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention buying raw materials in bulk in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that raw materials are usually the single largest cost for most manufacturing firms.
options.B: To show that large firms pay more for their inputs than small ones.
options.C: To explain how firms decide which suppliers to work with.
options.D: To give an example of a source of the cost advantages of large-scale production.

questions.explanation: Bulk buying is offered as one of the "several distinct sources" of the declining cost curve, illustrating where scale advantages come from.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Spreading fixed costs over more units lowers the average cost.
options.B: Average costs keep falling no matter how large a firm becomes.
options.C: Bulk purchasing can lower a large firm's costs.
options.D: Very large firms may become harder to coordinate.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "beyond a certain point average costs begin to rise again," so option B is not supported; the others are all stated.

items.passage:
A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed by it, so that the same small amount can act again and again. Long before the process was understood, artisans relied on catalysts unknowingly, using yeast to ferment bread and metal surfaces to hasten the making of acids. These early practitioners saw only that certain added materials changed how fast a reaction ran, without knowing why.

This puzzle drew the attention of chemists in the early nineteenth century. In 1836 the Swedish chemist Jons Jacob Berzelius grouped these scattered observations under a single name, catalysis, proposing that a mysterious 'force' released by the catalyst drove the reactions. This idea named the phenomenon but explained little, treating the catalyst's power as an unexplained property rather than a describable mechanism.

This vague notion was gradually replaced by a mechanical account rooted in how molecules interact. Modern chemists showed that a catalyst provides an alternative pathway for a reaction, lowering the energy barrier that reacting molecules must overcome, which allows the reaction to proceed far more quickly. However, predicting in advance which substance will catalyze a given reaction remains difficult, and much industrial catalyst design still depends on trial and error.
paragraphs: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed by it, so that the same small amount can act again and again. Long before the process was understood, artisans relied on catalysts unknowingly, using yeast to ferment bread and metal surfaces to hasten the making of acids. These early practitioners saw only that certain added materials changed how fast a reaction ran, without knowing why.

This puzzle drew the attention of chemists in the early nineteenth century. In 1836 the Swedish chemist Jons Jacob Berzelius grouped these scattered observations under a single name, catalysis, proposing that a mysterious 'force' released by the catalyst drove the reactions. This idea named the phenomenon but explained little, treating the catalyst's power as an unexplained property rather than a describable mechanism.

This vague notion was gradually replaced by a mechanical account rooted in how molecules interact. Modern chemists showed that a catalyst provides an alternative pathway for a reaction, lowering the energy barrier that reacting molecules must overcome, which allows the reaction to proceed far more quickly. However, predicting in advance which substance will catalyze a given reaction remains difficult, and much industrial catalyst design still depends on trial and error.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The specific experiments performed by Jons Jacob Berzelius.
options.B: How the understanding of catalysts developed from practical use to a scientific explanation.
options.C: The use of yeast and metal surfaces in early craft production.
options.D: The reasons that industrial catalysts are difficult to design.

questions.explanation: The passage traces catalysts from unknowing practical use, through Berzelius's naming, to the modern mechanistic account, covering the whole development of the concept.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what did Berzelius propose about catalysis?

options.A: That catalysts are consumed during the reactions they speed up.
options.B: That a special force released by the catalyst caused the reactions.
options.C: That catalysis occurred only within living organisms.
options.D: That catalysts worked by raising the temperature of the mixture.

questions.explanation: Berzelius proposed "that a mysterious 'force' released by the catalyst drove the reactions."

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It provides further evidence supporting Berzelius's idea of a catalytic force.
options.B: It repeats the practical craft examples introduced in paragraph 2.
options.C: It replaces the vague explanation of paragraph 2 with a mechanistic one.
options.D: It questions whether catalysts speed up reactions at all.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 begins "This vague notion was gradually replaced by a mechanical account," so it supersedes Berzelius's unexplained "force" with a described mechanism.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the energy barrier of a reaction?

options.A: A higher energy barrier makes a reaction proceed more slowly.
options.B: Lowering the energy barrier slows a reaction down.
options.C: The energy barrier depends on the temperature of the room.
options.D: Reactions with a barrier cannot occur without a catalyst.

questions.explanation: Since lowering the barrier lets a reaction "proceed far more quickly," a higher, unlowered barrier must make the reaction go more slowly.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, why is designing industrial catalysts still difficult?

options.A: Because catalysts are consumed too quickly to be reused.
options.B: Because most useful catalysts are too expensive to produce.
options.C: Because industrial reactions occur at very high pressures.
options.D: Because it is hard to predict which substance will catalyze a given reaction.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "predicting in advance which substance will catalyze a given reaction remains difficult," leaving design to trial and error.
